coredata.dc:title: Preparation of microfluidic-based pectin microparticles loaded carbon dots conjugated with BMP-2 embedded in gelatin-elastin-hyaluronic acid hydrogel scaffold for bone tissue engineering application
coredata.dc:creator: Rajabnejad keleshteri, Alireza
Moztarzadeh, Fathollah
Farokhi, Mehdi
Mehrizi, Ali Abouei
Basiri, Hamideh
Mohseni, Seyed Sepehr
coredata.dc:description: The controlled delivery of the bone morphogenetic protein-2 (BMP-2) with tracking ability would overcome most of the side effects linked to the burst release and uncontrolled delivery of this growth factor for bone regeneration. Herein, BMP-2-conjugated carbon dots (CDs) was used as noninvasive detection platforms to deliver BMP-2 for therapeutic applications where osteogenesis and bioimaging are both required. With this in mind, the present work aimed to develop a controlled BMP-2-CDs release system using composite scaffolds containing BMP-2-CDs loaded pectin microparticles, which had been optimized for bone regeneration. By using microfluidic approach, we encapsulated BMP-2-CDs in pectin microparticles with narrow size distribution and then incorporated into composite scaffolds composed of gelatin, elastin, and hyaluronic acid. The BMP-2-CDs was released from the composite scaffolds in a sustained fashion for up to 21 days exhibited a high controlled delivery capacity. When tested in vitro with MG-63 cells, these extraction mediums showed the intercellular uptake of BMP-2-CDs and enhanced biological properties and pro-osteogenic effect. By utilizing the pectin microparticles carrying BMP-2-CDs as promising bioimaging agents for growth factor delivery and by tuning the composition of the scaffolds, this platform has immense potential in the field of bone tissue regeneration.

The droplet phase contained 2 wt% pectin solution mixtures with BMP-2-CDs (at BMP-2 concentration of 1 μg/mL and CD concentration of 100 μg/mL). head.ce:title: Preparation of microfluidic-based pectin microparticles loaded carbon dots conjugated with BMP-2 embedded in gelatin-elastin-hyaluronic acid hydrogel scaffold for bone tissue engineering application

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The controlled delivery of the bone morphogenetic protein-2 (BMP-2) with tracking ability would overcome most of the side effects linked to the burst release and uncontrolled delivery of this growth factor for bone regeneration. Herein, BMP-2-conjugated carbon dots (CDs) was used as noninvasive detection platforms to deliver BMP-2 for therapeutic applications where osteogenesis and bioimaging are both required. With this in mind, the present work aimed to develop a controlled BMP-2-CDs release system using composite scaffolds containing BMP-2-CDs loaded pectin microparticles, which had been optimized for bone regeneration. By using microfluidic approach, we encapsulated BMP-2-CDs in pectin microparticles with narrow size distribution and then incorporated into composite scaffolds composed of gelatin, elastin, and hyaluronic acid. The BMP-2-CDs was released from the composite scaffolds in a sustained fashion for up to 21 days exhibited a high controlled delivery capacity. When tested in vitro with MG-63 cells, these extraction mediums showed the intercellular uptake of BMP-2-CDs and enhanced biological properties and pro-osteogenic effect. By utilizing the pectin microparticles carrying BMP-2-CDs as promising bioimaging agents for growth factor delivery and by tuning the composition of the scaffolds, this platform has immense potential in the field of bone tissue regeneration.

ce:section.ce:para: Therapeutic biomacromolecules in bone tissue regeneration, such as growth factors, have been used to promote the regeneration of bone-related disease by stimulating cellular activities and expression of osteogenic markers [1,2]. Bone morphogenetic protein-2 (BMP-2) has impressive potential as an osteogenic growth factor, useful in robust and functional bone healing in critical-size defects [3]. It has been shown that BMP-2 be present in the various stages of bone healing, from the initial phases of defect repair to later stages, including osteogenesis [4].

Several cases of bone injuries occur that result in impaired, irregular, or no repair promotion of the bone [5,6]. In these cases, using a substrate with the ability of local and sustained release of bone-specific growth factors at injured sites is the proper strategy to repair non-union bone defects. Nevertheless, there is a great need for drug delivery systems that allow for control the protein tracking to enhance their therapeutic efficacy. Similar to bioimaging for tumor therapy or angiogenic therapy, biocompatible fluorescence imaging will also benefit for in situ tracking of growth factors in the aspect of providing a more accurate estimation of the growth factor release in the targeted area [7,8]. Therefore, nanocarriers combining fluorescent property with imaging and tracking BMP-2 ability can serve as an ideal growth factor carrier in bone tissue engineering.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) with ultra-small sizes are emerging stars in the field of drug delivery [9,10]. Among various fluorescence materials, CDs have attracted impressive attention because of biocompatibility [11], high water dispersibility [12], and remarkable photoluminescence properties [13]. Surface modification flexibility of CDs with plenty of functional groups makes CDs as an attractive choice in conjugation with drugs [14,15]. In the recent literatures, several reports are focused on the applications of CDs conjugated with proteins, anticancer drugs, and antibodies with high cell membrane permeability [16,17]. In current study, BMP-2-conjugated CDs (BMP-2-CDs) was used for bioimaging and tracking of growth factor delivery. Besides, sustained release and long persistence of BMP-2 undergoing the treatment is critically important because the rapid release of BMP-2 from its substrate has been related to several side effects [18–20]. Till now, several carriers have been introduced for the delivery of various bioactive molecules. For example, micro and nanoparticles are appropriate candidates for various drug delivery purposes [21,22]. Further, there are many methods for fabrication of micro and nanoparticles with various morphologies and size [23]. Among them, microfluidic (MF) techniques are utilized as a versatile tool for generating monodisperse and uniform droplets, which is significant for a controlled drug delivery system [24,25]. Besides, MF can control the size of the droplets with narrow size distribution for desired applications [26,27]. Pectin is a reliable polysaccharide that can be used as an encapsulating material and is classified as an excellent candidate for encapsulation of proteins, cells, and, genes which makes pectin a suitable carrier for drug delivery [28,29].

Ideally, these loaded microparticles would ensure the stability of the BMP-2 during the fabrication process and allow its controlled delivery after incorporation in the scaffold. Also, ideal scaffold matrices should fulfill requirements with appropriate mechanical properties, proper biocompatibility, pore architecture, and controlled biodegradability [30].

Gelatin (Gel) as a hydrolyzed collagen has been extensively utilized for pharmaceutical and regenerative medicine applications due to its biosafety [31]. Furthermore, using biodegradable gelatin-based scaffolds has been recommended as tissue engineered constructs to form new ECM by the cells [32].

Elastin (E) is also a highly elastic protein polymer that can act as a key for the proper function of tissues, such as bone and cartilage, due to good biological performance [33]. In particular, elastin holds remarkable potential owing to its role in resisting compressive loads, long-term stability, and high elasticity to support tissue regeneration under physiological mechanical loads [34,35].

Hyaluronic acid (HA), as an attractive polysaccharide polymer, is a viscoelastic hydrogel that can provide an environment that promotes cell motility, cell-cell interaction, and proliferation in injured tissues [36,37]. HA is well recognized as a non-immunogenic hydrogel material used as a component in composite scaffolds to better mimic ECM for tissue engineering applications [38,39].

There is still much scope for the further tune the physical properties of scaffolds constructed of gelatin (G), gelatin-elastin (G-E), gelatin-hyaluronic acid (G-HA), and gelatin-elastin-hyaluronic acid (G-E-HA) to improve the ability of these composite hydrogels to better regenerate bone tissues.

In this study, a cell-penetrating BMP-2-CDs system was designed with the ability to imaging and delivery of BMP-2. Then, we presented a simple approach for the synthesis of the monodisperse pectin microparticles with a narrow size distribution in a droplet-based microfluidic system for delivery of BMP-2-CDs. To optimize the capacity of the scaffold in the physicochemical properties and cellular behavior, BMP-2-CDs loaded pectin microparticles were incorporated in the freeze-dried composite scaffolds based on gelatin-elastin-hyaluronic acid. The physical properties of the composite scaffolds, such as swelling, degradation, and compressive moduli, were evaluated. Furthermore, the BMP-2 release profile was assessed. Finally, biological responses of the effect of fabricated composite scaffolds on human osteosarcoma cell lines (MG-63 cells) in terms of biocompatibility, cellular uptake, cell attachment, alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity, and matrix mineralization production were investigated. We hypothesized that a G-E-HA scaffold containing BMP-2-CDs loaded pectin microparticles could be a potential candidate for the regeneration of bone defects.

Glucose, L-arginine (98%), human BMP-2, hydrogen chloride (HCl), mineral oil, Span 80, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), phosphate buffered saline (PBS), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), and gelatin (Type A, from porcine skin, 300 g Bloom) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Canada. Elastin with molecular weight 60 kDa (Elastin-Soluble, No. ES12) was provided from Elastin Products Company, Inc., USA. Sodium hyaluronate (Research Grade, 500–749 kDa) was supplied by Lifecore Biomedical, USA. Milli-Q grade deionized water (DI) was used in all experiments. The surfactant Triton X-100 and glacial acetic acid were supplied by Emd Millipore and Acros Organics, USA, respectively. Dialysis tubing with molecular weight cutoff 14,000 and 100 kDa was supplied by Thermo Scientific. Citrus pectin (galacturonic acid ≥ 74.0%), zinc nanopowder (40–60 nm avg. part. size, ≥99.0%), surfactant Titron X-100, glacial acetic acid, 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and [3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide] (MTT) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. SU-8 photoresist was purchased from MicroChem. Poly-(dimethylsiloxane) (PDMS) (Sylgard 184) was supplied by Dow Corning. BMP-2 enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) kit was purchased from R&D Systems, USA. ALP's kit was purchased from Pars Azmun, Iran. Human osteoblast-like MG-63 cell lines were obtained from the National Cell Bank, Iran.

For synthesis of CDs, 0.36 g of glucose and 0.36 g arginine were dispersed in 18 mL of DI water in a glass beaker. After stirring for 30 min to obtain a clear solution, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. After that, the chamber was sealed and put into an oven. Upon completion of the hydrothermal reaction in the oven at 160 °C for 22 h, the reaction product was centrifuged at 8500 rpm for 15 min to remove the black precipitates after cooling to room temperature. The brown supernatant was collected and dialyzed using a 14,000 Da MWCO dialysis membrane for 24 h under stirring conditions against DI water in order to obtain high-quality CDs, and freeze-dried to yield the CDs solid sample. The resultant CDs were stored in the dark for subsequent use.

To conjugate BMP-2 to CDs, activated carboxyl groups of CDs were linked to the amine groups of BMP-2 via EDC/NHS mediated reaction. For this purpose, 0.8 mL of the aqueous solution of CDs was added to an aqueous EDC solution (20 mM) and stirred at ambient temperature for 30 min. Next, 0.4 mL of NHS (20 mM) was added to the mixture and stirred for another 30 min. Then, BMP-2 solution in PBS with a concentration of 1 μg/mL was added to same flask containing the mixture and the resulting solution was allowed to stir for 4 h at 37 °C. Finally, to remove free unconjugated BMP-2, the BMP-2-CDs conjugates solution was purified by dialysis through a membrane (100 kDa MWCO) against PBS buffer.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the CDs and BMP-2-CDs were recorded using a Philips CM-300 microscope operating at 200 kV. The TEM samples were taken by deposition of the aqueous solution of CDs and BMP-2-CDs in thin films on a 200 mesh carbon film-coated copper grid. The UV–vis absorption of the prepared CDs and BMP-2-CDs were characterized by UV–vis spectroscopy in a 1 cm cell using a Cary 5000 UV–Vis–NIR spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) emission features of the CDs and BMP-2-CDs from the previous step were measured by a Cary Eclipse Varian fluorescence spectrophotometer. Attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) was conducted to characterize the CDs, BMP-2 and BMP-2-CDs by a PerkinElmer spectrum 100 FTIR spectrometer.

The MG-63 cell viability after treatment with an aqueous solution of CDs and BMP-2-CDs was examined using MTT assay. For this purpose, 100 μL of 1 × 104 cells/mL MG-63 cells in a DMEM medium supplemented with 10% (w/v) FBS was first harvested in a 96-well plate and incubated in incubator. The culture media were exchanged with 100 μL of fresh medium containing CDs at a concentration varying from 0 to 200 μg/mL for dose optimization or BMP-2-CDs with fixed CDs concentration at 100 μg/mL and BMP-2 concentrations of 0.01, 0.1, 0.5, and 1 μg/mL. After 24 h incubation, MTT solution (100 μL, 0.5 mg/mL) was added to the wells, and the cells were incubated for another 4 h. The formazan, formed by mitochondria enzymes' activity, was solubilized with DMSO for 30 min to dissolve the formazan crystals formed. The absorbance of the solution in each well was measured at a wavelength of 570 nm using a microplate reader (Awareness Technology Inc., USA), and the relative cell viability was determined. The cells not treated with CDs and BMP-2-CDs were used as controls. This assay was repeated at least six times.

The concentration of BMP-2 in BMP-2-CDs was analyzed using a human BMP-2 Quantikine ELISA kit. ELISA was performed according to the manufacturer's instruction (R&D Systems, USA). The BMP-2 absorbance was measured by an ELISA reader at an optical wavelength of 450 nm and interpolated from the standard curve.

A microfluidic device was fabricated using a soft lithography procedure. Photolithographic masters were prepared from SU-8 photoresist in bas-relief on silicon wafers. Also, the MF devices were fabricated with PDMS with a curing agent. Before experiments, the MF device was maintained for 4 h in an oven at 80 °C. The replica layer was peeled off from the mold, and inlets/outlet holes were punched. This layer was then plasma treated for 90 s and bonded to a planar PDMS layer with a thickness of 3 mm.

BMP-2-CDs were encapsulated into pectin microparticles through an MF chip. To prepare pectin microparticles, 2 wt% pectin solutions served as the dispersed phase containing BMP-2-CDs, while mineral oil containing 3% w/v of the non-ionic surfactant span 80 served as the continuous phase. Both phases were individually injected into the microchannels, and following MF emulsification, the dispersed phase formed monodisperse droplets. The aqueous phase and oil phase were injected at the flow rate of 1 and 10 μL/min, respectively. The droplets exited the chip through the outlet tubing and collected in a zinc nanoparticle solution containing biocompatible Triton X-100 to crosslink into monodispersed microparticles. The resulting microparticles were centrifuged at 3500 rpm for 10 min, washed with a PBS buffer, and transferred into PBS buffer (pH 7.4). The size of the resultant microparticles was determined using microscope images and NIH image J software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of crosslinked G-E-HA scaffolds containing BMP-2-CDs loaded pectin microparticles
ce:section.ce:para: A 8 wt% G solution was dissolved in DI water at 40 °C. A 2 wt% E aqueous solution and a 0.5 wt% HA aqueous solution were prepared. Composite scaffolds were prepared via lyophilization. Samples of G, G-E, G-HA, and G-E-HA were prepared and stirred with a magnetic bar at 50 °C for 60 min. After preparing the mixed solution, 10 mg BMP-2-CDs pectin microparticles were added and stirred for 15 min to achieve uniform dispersion. Each group quickly froze at −70 °C overnight and lyophilized at −50 °C for 36 h using pre-formed molds. The scaffolds obtained were cross-linked by immersion in 90% (v/v) ethanol containing 30 mM EDC and 8 mM NHS at 4 °C. After reaction at room temperature for 2 h, the scaffolds were washed with a large amount of DI water (with 5 changes of washing solution) to remove residual EDC and then freeze-dried in the same conditions.

The morphology of the pectin microparticle was evaluated under the environmental scanning electron microscope (FEI ESEM QUANTA 200, USA) at an accelerating voltage of 15 kV. Before imaging, 20 μL of microparticles suspension were placed on an aluminum foil, placed on the SEM sample holder, and sputter-coated with gold under vacuum. The elemental mapping of the microparticles was investigated by an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (Silicone Drift 2017, USA). To observe the scaffolds' morphology, a small part of them were coated with gold and examined using ESEM (FEI ESEM QUANTA 200, USA) and evaluated at different magnifications.

The porosity of the scaffolds was determined by liquid displacement. Scaffolds with and without pectin microparticles were cut into 5 × 5 × 1 mm pieces, placed in a 10 mL cylinder containing a predetermined volume of ethanol (V1). The porosity of the scaffold was expressed by Eq. (1):
(1)
Porosity =
V 1
−
V 3
/
V 2
−
V 3
× 100
where V2 is the total volume of ethanol and ethanol-impregnated scaffolds, and V3 is the residual ethanol volume after removing ethanol-impregnated scaffolds.

For in vitro degradation assay, the initial weight (W0) of all the hydrogel scaffolds with and without pectin microparticles were measured and then incubated in PBS at 37 °C. After different time intervals, the degradation rates of the scaffolds were calculated using Eq. (2):
(2)
Degradation rate
%
=
W 0
−
W t
/
W 0
× 100 %
where W0 and Wt represent initial weight (mg) and final weight of the sample (mg), respectively.

The swelling behavior of hydrogels was monitored by measuring water uptake. The initial weight of each freeze-dried sample was accurately measured, and then they were placed in PBS in a thermostatic bath at 37 °C. Samples were removed after 24 h, excess surface water was gently removed using a filter paper, and after being weighed, were re-immersed in PBS. The percentage swelling ratio (SR %) at each time point was calculated using Eq. (3):
(3)
SR
%
=
W t
−
W 0
/
W 0
× 100 %
where Wt is the weight of each swollen sample at different times and W0 is the initial weight of each sample.

The compressive strength of cylindrical scaffolds (Ø 10 mm × 12 mm) was subjected to compression tests using a mechanical testing machine (Santam, STM 20, Iran) with a crosshead speed of 0.5 mm/min under ambient conditions. At least five identical samples per group were measured to calculate the average and standard deviation.

The amount of BMP-2 loaded on hydrogel scaffolds were determined by BMP-2 Quantikine ELISA Kit. To evaluate the amount of released BMP-2 from G, G-E, G-HA, and G-E-HA hydrogel scaffolds, specimens were soaked in 10 mL PBS at 37 °C. The supernatant was collected and replaced with fresh PBS at 1, 3, 7, 14, and 21 days. The concentration of BMP-2 released from the scaffolds was measured with the aforementioned solid-phase sandwich ELISA kit following the manufacturer's instruction. The absorbance of BMP-2 was determined using an ELISA microplate reader at a wavelength of 450 nm.

The scaffolds' biocompatibility was evaluated through an indirect test. The scaffolds' extraction solution was carried out according to ISO 10993-5 guideline. Accordingly, the scaffolds with a surface area of 1 cm2 were soaked in 1 mL of culture medium and incubated at 37 °C in 5% CO2 for days 1, 3, 7, and 14 (the extraction solution of all scaffolds at day 14 was used for ALP and matrix mineral production studies). The MG-63 cells used for the MTT assay. The cells were plated at a density of 1 × 104 cells/well in 96-well plates at 37 °C. After 24 h of culturing, the scaffold extracted solutions were added in each well with a fresh complete culture medium. After 24 h incubation, 100 μL of MTT dye solution (0.5 mg/mL in PBS at pH 7.4) was added to each well, and the plates were incubated at 37 °C for 4 h. The isopropanol was used to dissolve the precipitated formazan for 30 min and precipitated formazan and mixed to dissolve the reacted dye. Finally, the absorbance of each well was determined at OD 570 nm in an ELISA plate reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro imaging and evaluation of cellular uptake
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the BMP-2-CDs potential application in cellular imaging, the uptake of the BMP-2-CDs released from G, G-E, G-HA, and G-E-HA scaffolds containing BMP-2-CDs loaded pectin microparticles was visualized using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) (Nikon Eclipse Ti-E, Japan). The MG-63 cells (5 × 104 cells/well in a logarithmic growth phase) were selected and seeded in a six-well plate. After incubation in DMEM for 24 h, the medium was removed, and cells were washed and fixed with 4% (w/v) paraformaldehyde for 2 h. Then, the cells were treated with the extraction medium of each group in a humidified 5% CO2-containing atmosphere at 37 °C. The cells were then allowed to incubate for 3 h and then washed three times with PBS. The cells were co-stained with of 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) solution for 20 min following washing with PBS. The CLMS was used to show intracellular localization of BMP-2-CDs. The fluorescence excitation of DAPI and released BMP-2-CDs was studied at 403, 488, 568, and 640 nm, respectively.

To assess the cellular morphology, and adhesion of MG-63 cells on the scaffolds, 2 × 105 cells were seeded on the surface of the disk-shaped scaffolds in a 24-well culture plate. After 4 h, 2 mL DMEM/F12 supplemented with 10% FBS was added to each well and incubated for another 24 h at 37 °C and 5% CO2. Subsequently, the attached cells were fixed in the buffer containing 4% glutaraldehyde for 90 min and then washed with PBS twice to remove residual glutaraldehyde. Then, the samples were dehydrated in graded aqueous ethanol (30%–100%) for 10 min. Finally, the scaffolds were sputter-coated with gold for further ESEM imaging.

ALP as a marker of osteoblast cells was measured by pNPP hydrolysis using a kinetic ALP assay. As the same steps for MTT assay, the cells were seeded in the extraction solutions of days 3, 7, and 14 of the scaffolds in 24-well tissue culture plates for 7 days. 20 μL of cell supernatant was added to 1000 μL of ALP's kit according to the manufacturer's instruction at 37 °C to evaluate the ALP production. The rate of the change in the optical density at 405 nm was measured using a microplate reader.

Alizarin red S staining was performed to evaluate the extracellular matrix mineralization and calcification nodules produced by osteoblast cells. The MG-63 cells (2 × 105 cells/well) were seeded on 24-well tissue culture plates and allowed to adhere for 24 h. Then, the supernatant medium was replaced with the extraction solutions of day 14 of the scaffolds. Finally, the MG-63 cells were washed with 0.1 NaCl and fixed in 1% paraformaldehyde for 30 min and then stained with 2% alizarin red solution. The stained areas were identified by light microscope and images were recorded. The results were qualitatively analyzed by the staining intensity and the extension of the positively AR-stained areas, i.e., red spots.

The quantitative results were expressed as means ± standard deviation (SD) for n ≥ 3. Multiple comparison analysis was conducted using the analysis of variance (ANOVA) technique to assess the statistical significance of the results. P-values below 0.05 were considered to be statistically significant.

Osteogenic growth factors are the prominent players in the treatment of bone defects. Hence, to mimic the natural bone repair, growth factors like BMP-2 as a vital factor need to be delivered in a similar spatiotemporal fashion to effectively induce bone formation and osteoblastic differentiation [40,41]. Embedding BMP-2 within engineering tissue constructs is an effective approach to stimulate new bone formation and osteoinductive properties [42–44]. Hence, the key to harnessing their reparative potential is to design a system to track the release and delivery of BMP-2 to mimic that of natural bone healing. In this study, CDs were synthesized based on our previously reported procedure with cell-tracking potential and reserved protein functionality [7]. Here, we report the development of BMP-2 conjugated CDs with intrinsic fluorescence properties owing to the incorporated CDs. To achieve high entrapment efficiency of BMP-2 microparticles, this work presents microfluidic encapsulation of BMP-2-CDs into pectin microparticles to control the released dose of BMP-2, and finally, they easily blend with the different polymer solution to prepare an optimized bone scaffold.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of synthesized CDs and BMP-2-CDs conjugates
ce:section.ce:para: Water-soluble, highly stable and one-step facile procedure of preparing strongly fluorescent CDs were obtained by hydrothermal treatment of glucose in the presence of arginine. The excellent colloidal stability of the CDs' aqueous dispersion was highly stable for several weeks in simulated physiological environments such as cell medium and PBS.

The morphology and particle size of CDs and BMP-2-CDs were examined by TEM, as shown in Fig. 1A, B, revealed that the CDs are highly dispersed and regularly round-shaped with an average diameter of between 3 and 6 nm, while the size of BMP-2-CDs was in the range of 7–10 nm. Based on the particle size distribution histogram in Fig. 1A, B (inset), the CDs and BMP-2-CDs had a mean particle size of approximately 4.3 nm and 8.5 nm. Fig. 1C shows UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of the aqueous CDs solution. The UV–vis absorption showed two prominent peaks; one at 262 nm due to π–π* transition of CC and another at 331 nm due to the n–π* transition of CO [12,45]. From the PL spectrum, an emission peak at 445 nm was observed under 360 nm excitation. To examine the possible application of CDs and BMP-2-CDs in multicolor bioimaging, the fluorescence properties were evaluated by investigating their PL behavior at series of excitation wavelengths (Fig. S1A, Supporting information). The emissions were excitation wavelength-dependent, and PL intensity reached maximum values when excited at 360 nm and then decreased after a peak maximum. These emission color changes suggest that the optical properties of CDs were dominated by the surface state [46,47]. Furthermore, the detailed PL investigation of BMP-2-CDs at different excitation wavelengths shows the excitation peaks of BMP-2 at 300 nm and CDs at 360 nm present simultaneously, and this confirms that the BMP-2-CDs contain both BMP-2 and CDs fluorescence features (Fig. S1B, Supporting information).

Fig. 1D presents the pH-dependent PL spectra of the CDs in a PBS solution on various pH values from 2.0 to 12.0. At acidic pH, the remaining carboxylic acid groups are protonated and cause a decline in PL intensity of CDs solution due to CDs aggregation and an obvious fluorescence quenching was observed [45]. A maximum value reached a pH of 7.0, which is suitable for biological applications. However, at basic pH, the carboxylic acid group of CDs is deprotonated (COO-), and the charged electron of the surface carboxyl groups of CDs forms a negative charge shell resulted in decreased fluorescence properties of CDs due to the suppression of electronic transitions in emissive surface states [48,49].

The UV–vis absorption of CDS, BMP-2, and BMP-2-CDs were compared in Fig. 1E. The UV–Vis spectral differences indicate the successful covalent conjugation of the BMP-2 to CDs nanoparticles. The BMP-2-CDs emerged a new absorption peak at 258 nm, which did not exist when tested separately in the same range, and CDs were easily decorated with BMP-2 via the reaction between the primary amine groups of BMP-2 and carboxylic groups of CDs.

Fig. 1F shows the PL spectra of BMP-2-CDs, individual BMP-2, and CDs' aqueous solutions. The presence of BMP-2 leads to quenching of fluorescence emission of CDs, which could be explained by covalent interaction between the CDs and BMP-2, demonstrating the formation of BMP-2-CDs conjugates. The redshift of the emission wavelength indicates that the microenvironment of tryptophan moieties in the BMP-2 could be more exposed to the hydrophilic environment influenced by the proximity of polar groups of CDs [50,51].

The MTT assays were performed to evaluate the viability of the MG-63 cells of the synthesized CDs and BMP-2-CDs. Mean absorbance for free CDs and BMP-2-CDs dose was expressed as a percentage of the control untreated well absorbance and plotted vs. CDs and BMP-2 concentration. The cell viability after 24 h exposure via various concentrations of CDs is shown in Fig. S2A, Supporting information. The average cell viability of CDs was above 80% after culturing with CDs, and that 100 μg/mL CDs were chosen as a safe dose for MG-63 cells at the concentration of 100 μg/mL. Cell viability in the presence of BMP-2-CDs with a fixed concentration of CDs at 100 μg/mL and various concentrations of BMP-2 were examined compared to the blank culture medium as a control group (Fig. S2B, Supporting information). The free BMP-2 solution significantly promoted the proliferation of MG-63 cells. Likewise, the biocompatibility of CDs and free BMP-2, no cytotoxicity of BMP-2-CDs has been observed against MG-63 cells, and all of those cells are alive at different concentrations. Thus, we then introduced the BMP-2-CDs conjugates as an effective protein imaging platform with negligible cytotoxicity in biological systems.

The ATR-FTIR spectroscopy was performed to analyze the functional groups present on the surface of CDs and the presence of the BMP-2 on conjugated BMP-2-CDs (Fig. S3, Supporting information). The synthesized CDs show the main absorption band of stretching vibrations of CO at 1698 cm−1 and stretching vibrations of CC at 1600 cm−1. The peaks at 2890 and 2982 cm−1 are related to the CH and =C-H bond stretching vibrations [52,53]. The occurrence of the amide bonds, ranging from 1600 to 1700 cm−1, from the stretching vibrations of the CO and CN groups in the amide I, and from 1580 to 1510 cm−1 from NH bending of amide II proved the presence of BMP-2 on the surface of the CDs [54]. An insignificant shift of these characteristic bands for BMP-2 at 1545 cm−1 was displaced to 1558 cm−1, compared to BMP-2, which occurs for BMP-2-CDs. The slight shift of these characteristic bands indicated forming a new amide bond between CDs and BMP-2.

The relative binding affinity of BMP-2 antibodies against BMP-2 characterized by ELISA technique. The BMP-2 and BMP-2-CDs had similar BMP-2 concentration levels of 1006 ng/mL. The BMP-2 maintains its binding affinity and specificity for anti-BMP-2 upon conjugation with CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation of monodispersed BMP-2-CDs loaded pectin microparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Monodispersed BMP-2-CDs loaded pectin droplets were generated from an MF flow-focusing droplet generator. The flow-focusing geometry of the MF device was employed to fabricate droplets under the shear stress imposed by the oil phase. The detailed design of the flow-focusing MF device is illustrated in Fig. S4, Supporting information. For the pectin droplets formation, 2 wt% pectin aqueous phase mixed with BMP-2-CDs supplied as the dispersed phase and mineral oil mixed with 3 wt% Span 80 were used as the continuous phase. The surfactant was added into the mineral oil phase to prevent the potential droplets merge and stabilized the formed droplets into spherical shapes. In the flow–focusing region, the oil phase from two symmetric side-channels sheared the aqueous fluids from the central channel into uniform droplets. Fig. 2A shows the microscopic images of the generation of monodispersed pectin droplets at the fixed flow rate of the oil (Qoil) at 10 μL/min and pectin solution (Qpec) at 1 μL/min. Droplets were collected by the beaker containing zinc nanoparticle solution mixed with biocompatible Triton X-100 as a crosslinker solution. Fig. 2B shows the representative optical microscopy image of the highly uniform pectin microparticles with an average diameter of 35 μm and the low coefficients of variation (<5%). The ionic gelation between pectin and zinc ions (Zn2+) occurred when the pectin droplets were dripped into the zinc nanoparticle solution. When Zn2+ diffused onto the surfaces and into the bodies of pectin droplets, the cross-linking of Zn2+ and carboxylic groups of pectin could form cross-linked structures on the microparticles, finally leading to the formation of complete microgels. Fig. 2C shows the size distribution of pectin microparticles and indicated that a highly narrowed size distribution is obtainable by using the microfluidic approach. The successful incorporation of BMP-2-CDs into the pectin microparticles can be confirmed by the fluorescence microscope images of the BMP-2-CDs loaded pectin microparticles (Fig. 2D).

The pectin microparticles produced with microfluidic technique had an acceptable uniformity with the size of ~50 μm as shown in SEM image (Fig. S5, Supporting information). The monodispersed spherical pectin microparticles implied the applications of appropriate preparation of the pectin microparticles. Moreover, the SEM-EDAX elemental mapping analysis of the pectin microparticles has been carried out. Fig. S4B shows the elemental maps of zinc along with the corresponding SEM image. These images several that zinc is uniformly distributed in the cross-linked pectin matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and porosity of scaffolds containing BMP-2-CDs loaded pectin microparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of the lyophilized G, G-E, G-HA, and G-E-HA scaffolds with and without BMP-2-CDs loaded pectin microparticles are shown in Fig. 3 . In all groups, the scaffolds showed porous structure with interconnected pores, which is appropriate for cell adhesion with enough space for cells ingrowth and facilitates the nutrition and oxygen transportation (Fig. 3A–D). The results obtained from the porosity measurement demonstrated that G, G-E, G-HA, and G-E-HA scaffolds had porosities of 86.7, 74.1, 89.3, and 80.8% and were embedded with BMP-2-CDs loaded pectin microparticles had porosities of 84.4, 70.3, 89.9, and 83.1, respectively. Also, SEM micrographs of G, G-E, G-HA, and G-E-HA scaffolds embedded with BMP-2-CDs loaded pectin microparticles showed that microparticles were generally uniformly distributed on the pore walls (Fig. 2E–H).

The swelling degree of the scaffolds embedded with the pectin microparticles was investigated after 24 h of incubation in PBS, as shown in Fig. 4A. The hydrogel scaffolds were expected to be used as the scaffolds of bone tissue engineering. Therefore, their water uptake abilities were critical for adjusting cell behaviors besides the transfer of cell nutrients and growth factors through the scaffolds [55]. In this study, the swelling behavior of composite hydrogel scaffolds was found to be tunable by varying the composition of the gel components. As expected, HA has a high capacity absorption capacity [38]. HA was incorporated in the G and G-E network because of their hygroscopic nature and expected to improve water uptake of the scaffolds [56]. The water uptake of the G-H scaffold was much higher than those of the G scaffold (Fig. 4A). The swelling ratio increased from 33.51 ± 1.14 in G to 47.61 ± 0.8 in the composite G-HA scaffold. On the other hand, the swelling ratios of G-E hydrogel scaffolds decreased to 25.89 ± 3.36 by adding 2 wt% E concentration. These results were expected because E is less hydrophilic than G and increasing polymer concentration allows for a higher degree of crosslinking through the hydrogel matrix, as previously reported [32]. Therefore, the resulting composite hydrogels possess a smaller pore size and induce less swelling compared to that of lower polymer concentrations. In the further composition, the swelling ratio of G-E-HA was 34.90 ± 1.03. Therefore, the combination of E and HA could be used to modulate the swelling behavior of hydrogel scaffolds. This tunable water uptake capacity promotes cell penetration and new autologous tissue ingrowth, which can be designed for tissue engineering application.

To determine in vitro hydrolytic degradation of G, G-E, G-HA, and G-E-HA scaffolds containing BMP-2-CDs loaded pectin microparticles, the scaffolds were incubated in PBS at 37 °C to evaluate the quantitative changes. The percentage of weight loss of hydrogel scaffolds as a function of degradation time was calculated and shown in Fig. 4B. The degradation pattern for all hydrogel scaffolds was found incremental during the investigation period, which proved scaffolds' ability to absorb liquids. It can be inferred from the results that the degradation rate is the highest for G-HA and the lowest for G-E samples. In particular, 55% of the G, 34% of the G-E, 60% of the G-HA, and 43% of the G-E-HA scaffolds were degraded during 21 days, suggesting that the degradation of the hybrid scaffolds decreased with the presence of E content in the G, and G-HA scaffolds due to the structural characteristics of E.

The mechanical properties of the scaffolds, in particular, those designed for bone tissue applications, are vital to support bone tissue regeneration effectively before biodegradation. The strength value of the freeze-dried scaffolds was measured using a compression testing machine to study the mechanical properties of the scaffolds embedded with pectin microparticles. As shown in Fig. 4C, the compressive strength was determined to be 3.80, 45.89, 27.95, and 38.23 MPa for G, G-E, G-HA, and G-E-HA, respectively.

The limitation of the G scaffolds is mainly due to their weakness in mechanical properties and quick degradation behavior. The scaffolds' mechanical properties could be optimized by composite scaffolds for the adaptation to the desired tissues and keep their stability during tissue formation. In the scaffolds containing E, the compressive modulus of scaffolds is enhanced due to the polymer concentration and more crosslinking density, which increases the mechanical properties. Previous studies showed that the presence of E in scaffolds could result in promoted mechanical features [34,35]. Furthermore, the addition of HA declined the compression modulus of the scaffolds from 45.89 for G-E to 38.23 MPa for G-E-HA. We ascribe this effect to the plasticization nature of hydrophilic HA, that made the scaffolds more flexible, and the scaffolds possess higher porosity [57,58]. The G-E-HA scaffold embedded with pectin microparticles was found to have favorable load-bearing properties for tissue engineering applications.

Fig. 4D shows the BMP-2 release profile from the pectin microparticles and embedded into the hydrogel scaffolds within 21 days in the PBS solution using ELISA kits. It is noticeable that the release rate of growth factor will be certainly affected by the microparticles' size [59]. The size of pectin microparticles is precisely controlled, and monodisperse size distributions were reached by the microfluidic approach. Therefore, uniform microparticles have similar water penetration and degradation, which can enhance the effect of controlled release.

The loading of the BMP-2 into pectin microparticles is obtained by the incorporation of the BMP-2 into the matrix of the polymer during microparticles production instead of immersion or physical adsorption of the growth factor onto the surface of the preformed microparticles. This loading approach had a significant effect on growth factor release, the more efficient binding of hydrogel with growth factor, and controlling its release from the hydrogel [60]. Fig. 4D shows the release profile of encapsulated BMP-2 from pectin microparticles alone and within hydrogel scaffolds over a period of 21 days. The release profile of BMP-2 from the pectin microparticles was characterized with a high release (more than 80%) during 14 days.

In contrast, the amounts of BMP-2 released from the scaffolds with embedded pectin microparticles were significantly lower than the pectin microparticles alone. After an initial burst release of 34, 16, 33, and 25% for G, G-E, G-HA, and G-E-HA, respectively on the first day, the BMP-2 was released in a sustained manner in the second stage due to the slow diffusion of the BMP-2 from the scaffold matrix. The additional barrier provided by the scaffolds, leading to a decrease in the rate of water permeation into scaffolds and BMP-2 diffusion. The release of BMP-2 of G hydrogel scaffold was faster than the composite G-E, G-HA, and G-E-HA scaffolds, as expected. It was observed that ~70% of BMP-2 was released for G scaffolds vs. ~55% G-E-HA scaffolds after 21 days of immersion. In particular, a comparison of the results of Fig. 4D with the degradation of four type hydrogel scaffolds (Fig. 4B) revealed that the release profile of the BMP-2 was associated with the degradation profile of the scaffolds. The release profile of BMP-2 could be controlled due to degradation and diffusion of the hydrogel composite scaffolds. Thus, we conclude that the composite scaffolds possess ideal properties for cell growth and tissue formation and their capability due to their growth factor delivery for tissue regeneration.

The MG-63 cell viability next to all types of scaffolds was determined by MTT assay. The number of cells had progressively increased in all scaffolds containing BMP-2-CDs loaded pectin microparticles by the passage of time. It is suggested that the presence of BMP-2 in the scaffolds had a positive effect on the proliferation rate of MG-63 cells. Besides, the cell proliferation in G-HA and G-E-HA scaffolds was higher during culture time compared with G and G-E scaffolds. Consistent with these observations, the cellular behavior is considerably influenced by the surrounding environment. The results confirmed that cellular behavior was sensitive to the composition, morphology, porosity, and mechanical strength of scaffolds (Fig. 5).

Growth factors' monitoring may offer a great potential to determine the health of healing grafts in bone tissue engineering. In vitro cellular uptake and intercellular distribution of the BMP-2-CDs released from G, G-E, G-HA, and G-E-HA scaffolds after 14 days were investigated by co-culturing with MG-63 for 3 h. As displayed in Fig. 6B, containing the nuclei with bright blue fluorescence DAPI greatly helps for the nucleus region indication. DAPI as good nucleus targeting imaging only excited at 403 nm. However, the BMP-2-CDs also targeted imaging the nucleus position and can be excited in wider excitation wavelength (488 and 640 nm). Moreover, the merged image of released BMP-2-CDs and DAPI is acutely strong evidence that BMP-2-CDs obtained both.

Noteworthy, excitation-dependent emission property of CDs caused multi-color emission with different excitation in all groups at 488 and 640 nm as detected by CLSM images in Fig. 6C, D. The BMP-2-CDs successfully translocated into cells via receptor-mediated endocytosis and demonstrated bright green and red fluorescence from BMP-2-CDs inside the cells. The cells presented no change in the morphology, indicating favorable living conditions, and no differences were observed in cellular uptake of BMP-2-CDs between all groups. The CLSM images revealed that the presence of CDs is a proper choice as a monitoring agent to deliver BMP-2 to cells and can be used as a tracking agent for therapeutic applications where osteogenesis and bioimaging are both required.

Fig. 7 manifests the SEM micrographs of the MG-63 cells proliferated on the surface of the hydrogel scaffolds containing BMP-2-CDs loaded pectin microparticles. The attaching ability of cells to the porous scaffolds, which mimic the structure of the extracellular matrix, is a significant favor in tissue engineering applications [59]. It is clear that purified MG-63 cells anchored their cytoplasmic filopodia to cover the four types of scaffold surface, but in the presence of HA in G-HA (Fig. 7C) and G-E-HA (Fig. 7D) a higher number of cells and better cell adhesion concerning the G and G-E scaffolds was observed. The presence of HA guides the unrestricted cell proliferation over the scaffolds. These results were in good agreement with previous studies, which suggest that HA-based matrices provide a favorable surface for cell adhesion and proliferation [36,39]. The high porosity interconnected scaffolds with suitable ability for cell penetration exhibits a proper environment for cell attachment and proliferation while preserving their morphologic properties. Thus, the G-E-HA containing BMP-2-CDs loaded pectin microparticles, showing more stability and a better environment for MG-63 cells spreading with potential application as biocompatible scaffolds for therapeutic and tissue engineering.

ALP activity assay was employed to assess the influence of the extraction solutions of G, G-E, G-HA, and G-E-HA scaffolds containing BMP-2-CDs loaded pectin microparticles on the osteogenesis of MG-63 cells. The tissue culture polystyrene (TCP) was considered as a negative control group. ALP production by MG-63 cells on days 3, 7, and 14, as an early marker for osteoblastic differentiation and matrix mineralization, was quantitatively evaluated, as presented in Fig. 8A. With the BMP-2-CDs presence in the hydrogel scaffolds, ALP activity increased significantly in comparison to TCP (P < 0.0001). An ascending trend in ALP activity was observed from day 7 to 14 in all four groups. Additionally, MG-63 cells had higher ALP production next to G-E-HA in comparison to other scaffolds. These data confirmed the results obtained from MTT assay that the inclusion of E and HA in scaffolds containing BMP-2-CDs, established a positive effect on the bone cell differentiation and mineralization process.

Fig. 8B shows the calcium deposition in MG-63 cultured within the extraction solutions of G, G-E, G-HA, and G-E-HA scaffolds containing BMP-2-CDs loaded pectin microparticles for 14 days culture period using Alizarin red S staining. Alizarin red S staining showed no mineralization in the TCP group (Fig. 8B) and low mineralization in the G group (Fig. 8C). In contrast, the MG-63 cells after incubation in extraction solution of G-E, G-HA, and G-E-HA scaffold showed bright red staining indicating extensive mineralization and significantly greater extent of bone nodules (Fig. 8D–F). In particular, scaffolds containing both E and HA revealed a higher amount of matrix mineralization as compared with other groups. Both E and HA exist as essential components of the ECM, and the addition of this substrate into bone tissue engineering scaffolds can promote osteoblastic cell functions like mineralization, osteogenic cell differentiation, and bone regeneration.

In the present study, biocompatible water-soluble CDs were developed by covalently conjugating with BMP-2 as a fluorescent tracking system. Fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–Vis, and IR supported successful BMP-2 attachment to the CDs nanoparticles. The as-prepared monodispersed BMP-2-CDs loaded pectin microparticles with controllable sizes were produced with a microfluidic chip. They were then placed in G, G-E, G-HA, and G-E-HA scaffolds. The microparticles were distributed uniformly within the porous construct and BMP-2-CDs were released in a sustained manner during 21 days. The nontoxic G-E-HA scaffold containing BMP-2-CDs loaded pectin microparticles showed an appropriate degradation, water uptake abilities, and mechanical strength besides the better cell attachment than other scaffolds because of the E and HA presence in the matrix. We also verified the cellular uptake of BMP-2-CDs released from scaffolds as a strong growth factor protein tracking in the drug delivery system. Moreover, ALP and matrix mineral production were expressed more in the G-E-HA scaffold containing BMP-2-CDs loaded pectin microparticles group. This study highlights the possibility of using the G-E-HA scaffold containing BMP-2-CDs encapsulated in well-designed microparticles with tracking ability as influential candidates for therapeutic and bone tissue engineering applications.

Alireza Rajabnejad Keleshteri: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - Original draft preparation. Fathollah Moztarzadeh: Supervision, Resources. Mehdi Farokhi: Methodology, Validation, Writing - Reviewing and editing. Ali Abouei Mehrizi: Validation, Writing - Reviewing and editing. Hamideh Basiri: Data curation, Methodology, Visualization, Writing - Original draft preparation. Seyed Sepehr Mohseni: Visualization, Investigation, Data curation.
